result:
The museum does not look like a museum. If you arrive by way of the low brick warehouse between the canal and the old tram depot, you will not find a gilded entrance or a map kiosk, no gift shop with postcards or enamel pins. You will find a door with a brass plate stamped with a name that changes as often as the weather—Institution for the Preservation of Transient Phenomena, Archive of Volatiles, the Smellery, depending on the mood of the Board and which young designer had their way this season. The door is rarely locked, because locks do nothing to deter true thieves, and the things we keep here can’t be pocketed like coins. Loss here is different. It is the loss of a roomful of air, the last breath of a winter, the way a city turned its face to the sea and inhaled once, around sundown, in 1986.

I am the one who keeps it.

Or I was, for a long time. For years, my hands were the ones that lifted ampoules from their nests, that sealed them with a tiny blue flame, that etched a code in wax on glass and laid it carefully in a drawer in the cool dark. If language is the house of being, as some philosopher said, then smell is the hidden garden behind it, all the gates ajar when you stumble upon them, and feral things moving in the shadows.

People come here to cry. They come to laugh sometimes, or to stand with their hands hovering, not yet ready to break the thin meniscus of cistern air that holds a summer afternoon on a grandfather’s porch or the interior of a photo lab in a basement, with trays of stop bath and the under-scent of rubber tubing. They come to see if what they remember is anything like what the world once was. They come to learn what a thing smelled like that they only heard about in old books: whale oil lamps, coal mornings, horse and iron and snow. And they leave with their own air around them changed, utterly mundane in the ways it still is—diesel, frying onions, street gutters, bleach—but cadenced differently, as if a new instrument has tuned itself just under their hearing.

On my first day as curator, the previous keeper presented me with a towel-wrapped bundle and said, “Do not drop this.” He had never been dramatic before. Inside the towel was a little ceramic jar, white as a tooth, waxed shut, and sealed again with a tiny length of red string, the kind that used to bind pastry boxes in a bakery that existed before wheat was milled for hunger and not joy. He put the jar in my hand and looked at me until I looked back.

“What is it?” I asked.

“Late winter, under the old oaks,” he said. “After the first thaw, and then the freeze again. Lichen and wet bark and the iron in the river. Listen,” he said. “You can feel the quiet of it through the porcelain.”

I laughed. Then I stopped. Of course he was right. The thing about smell is that it never really is what it is. It is always the thing-plus. It is a chord. Your brain does the work—forging the bridge between chemical patterns and memories, laying down roads and then forgetting you laid them, so you find yourself driving at dusk and suddenly, despite every reasonable fact, a place you haven’t been in twenty years is upon you, the road rising to meet it, just from the way someone in the back seat opened a jar of peppercorns.

The museum began as a joke, as so many serious things do. There were some chemists and some poets and a benefactor who had grown rich on fragrances—that is, on made smells, fantasies of citrus blossom and clean musk—and he had the idea that there should be a counterweight to fantasy, a place that collected the actual, before the actual slipped away. This was on the cusp, when no one was paying attention to ways of life that were about to end. There was a little listicle then, that people passed around and forgot, entitled Ten Smells Our Children Will Never Know. No one thought there would be a hundred, and then a thousand. No one thought we would begin recording what streets smelled like at certain hours because those hours would be taken by other kinds of time. No one thought we would have to explain that air used to smell like nothing, or like everything, depending on the day and your mother’s hand in yours.

We learned how to take air and make it hold still. Not the air itself, which is impossible and was never the point. We learned how to coax molecules out of it, to make them nestle in other things that would not let them slip away so quickly. We used little metal needles and special fibers and chilled glass. We could give you the exhale of old books—a mix of vanillin from lignin breaking down, a hint of benzaldehyde, that dusty note I only learned to call coumarin after the head chemist brought her plant handbook to lunch and made us sniff crushed tonka beans. We could give you the upstairs hallway of a farmhouse when boots were stacked on the mat and mittens hung on the line to dry, when the dog had trailed in snow and heat came up through the vent with the faint tang of iron. We could give you a boy’s pocket in the mid-1990s, if we wanted to, insofar as a pocket had a smell: synthetic grape lip balm, pennies, the vinyl of a Pokémon card sleeve, the starch of brand-new denim.

We wrote principles. We had to. The benefactor insisted on ethics, as if this were medicine and not art. You do not capture what still breathes in the ordinary—no stealing what is abundant, for the terror of absence will corrode your hands and make a museum greedier than a dragon. And you do not counterfeit. You do not suggest that you have held, in glass, the first breath of the first lover in the first gaze. Nostalgia is a thief with bright teeth; don’t hand it a crown. You clear the names of the dead where you can, because their kitchens live on in their grandchildren’s grief, and grief belongs to people, not to institutions. You say when you have failed, and you enshrine your failures, because sometimes the most honest thing you can say about a lost thing is that you missed it by an hour and the fog burned off and your jar filled only with air.

People wrote in. They sent us handkerchiefs, scarves, clods of dirt wrapped in waxed paper. They sent us hatchlings of their childhood rooms: a broken hairbrush with their mother’s hair still braided through, a kitchen timer shaped like a tomato, letter paper with a pale cloud of perfume that hung on for decades because it had been shut away from the sun in an armoire. Sometimes we sent those things back after recording them. Sometimes we kept them under legal agreements, in the dark in the stacks, where the air hums at a temperature that is less a number than a truce. Our cataloging system would have horrified a librarian. It was intuitive the way a spice rack is intuitive. We had drawers labeled Rain on Copper, Paper in Sun, Plastic When It Was Still Called Bakelite. We had a drawer, deep and locked, labeled War.

Once, a woman in a green scarf came to the desk and said, “Do you have 2008? The smell when you walked into a store and there were flat-screen televisions everywhere all showing the same scene of a mountain?” I had to ask her three times what she meant. When I did understand, I brought her to C-14, Electronics in the Age of Awe. I gave her the box of OLED. She did not cry. She gave a dry little laugh and then she sat on the bench and breathed for a long time, shoulders dropped, like someone who had been sprinting without knowing it.

The week after, a boy came. He could not have been more than nine. He stood there with fringe in his eyes and his hands jammed into his pockets like he might keep himself from falling out of them. “My father,” he said, serious as his shoes.

“I’m sorry,” I said, before he said what. When people here say a thing without saying it, it is usually about who is gone.

“He smelled like… I can’t say,” the boy said. “He worked nights. He said the night smelled different, that it had layers like a cake, and he could tell the time by noses. He would come home and he smelled like… like handles and something like new water,” the boy said, and I nodded, because yes, yes, I knew what he meant. He meant aluminum dawn and dehumidifiers and the faint ozone that oak trees give off when there is a storm somewhere else.

“We can’t give you your father,” I said. Ethics. We can give you a hallway that people like your father walked down at three in the morning that summer, and what that hallway smelled like. We can give you the keystones.

“I know,” he said. He was braver than I was, at nine.

I gave him the jar labeled Freight Elevator at Shift Change, Summer High Humidity. The museum knows its people. The boy smiled for the first time then, a slow unwinding of something wound too tight. He breathed. He left the jar closed. He took it home in his backpack like a lantern.

The core of everything I did was attention. The method was only method. If you want method, you can read the papers. There are general principles—do not breathe on the sample, no perfume on skin, do not let a banana sit in your bag if you are going to capture the smell of old coins; be aware that clothes carry laundry detergent like a flag, that a room in summer will offer up itself differently than in winter. There are special mousetraps for stray molecules. There is glass that holds the weightless as if it weighed something. There are little boxes of silica to keep water out. There are cold places and dark places, and places where both joy and rot sleep. And there is, always, the knowledge that what you do is an act of translation. When I opened a jar for someone, I never said, “This is the thing.” I said, “This is a way in.”

The trouble was winter. Always winter. Summers morphed, but they kept the rhythm of their rot and blossom. Autumns went rusty and damp and strange, but leaves still did what leaves do, and cinnamon hung at doorways in every language. Springs packed themselves into weeks and came at you all at once like a crowd that had been waiting between velvet ropes. But winter was the one that got thin, like a bedspread stretched to cover too many feet.

When I was a child, winter had several sub-winter smells. Early winter was wool and breath and static and cedar. Deep winter smelled like metal and pine resin under its bark and the particular silence when your eyelashes freeze and crack a little and every small sound is magnified because the air says, Everything that lives is sleeping, shh. Thaw smelled like something you never did get to catch, because by the time you turned around to ask for a jar it was gone. And then there was the smell of snow itself, that everyone insists has no smell, the way people insist the number zero is nothing when it is something vast.

My mother would open the door and put her face out and say, “It’s going to snow.” And then, very soon, it would. There is science for this, which is not magic but is close; there is a mix of ions and airborne particles and an absence of the world’s usual stink. There is the smell of traffic going quiet because everyone went home. There is a faint tang of ozone, and of some things that are always present but not always available to us—wet stone, the breath of things that the cold has sealed shut.

I had a jar on my desk the whole of my tenure. It was a ceramic one, like the one my predecessor had given me, but larger, with no label. The jar was empty. I kept it as a joke and as a talisman and as a reminder that, sometimes, the closest thing to being honest is leaving the room empty, and letting people stand there with their good memories and their bad ones, and not adding one mote to impress them.

I held off on trying to capture winter for as long as I could. People brought us their own attempts, and we did what we could with them. But I knew that one day I would have to go back to the place where my mother had opened doors to sniff the sky. And then the letter came.

It was handwritten, on paper that smelled like municipal storage: a faint goatishness and something like pencil eraser. The return address was the town office of the valley where my mother kept a house after my father left, in the shadow of the old sawmill. The letter was from a man whose name I recognized only when I read it twice. He had dated the librarian’s daughter when I was twelve and then gone to fish offshore and then come back and then disappeared again into the quiet industries of men who repair what other men break.

He said: There’s a week. Maybe two. The bog is going to be taken for the development, and the oaks we called the Listening Oaks are flagged for cutting. The weather has been false all winter, but last night it froze properly, and the river speaks like it used to. If you’re going to do it, do it now.

I showed the letter to the Board. They wrung their hands and talked about liability. I put the red string around the ceramic jar and threw two sets of fibers, three vials, and the old wool scarf I had not worn in years into my bag, and I went.

The valley was thinner than I remembered. You live long enough and everything becomes thinner: the soup, your patience, the air between people and the things they mean. The river was there. It was smaller. It lay like a mirror someone had breathed on and forgot to wipe clean. The mill was a ghost. The oaks were there, their bark like plated armor, the kind that would have revived the spirits of children who liked dragons. The lichen on them was still bright, like the crusts of pastry no one makes anymore, because it takes three days and butter is reserved for candles now.

Yaros, the man with the letter, met me at the edge of the field where he’d played baseball as a boy. “You got thin,” he said, by way of greeting.

“I always was,” I said, which was true. It was the world that had thickened and thinned around me in waves, not me.

We went to the river. It was a wrinkle of iron. The air was thirty-two degrees, then twenty-nine, then something like a note struck too high. He stopped me with a hand and said, “Listen to it.”

I did. The oak boughs creaked, not like anything was breaking but like something was realigning. Somewhere far off, someone’s door banged shut. Under everything was the muffled hiss that only snow knows. And there, there, it was, the thing that my mother called winter’s breath. The air leaned in like a person at a party who wants to tell you a secret. It smelled like nickel and clean paper and the unbred parts of a forest. It smelled like the curl of a wool mitten when it’s brand new, and not the way I had worn them as a child, soaked through and dried by the stove, over and over, until they belonged to the house as much as to me.

“Now,” Yaros said.

We set the fibers in the jar. We did not talk. When people talk about reverence, they think of churches. They do not think of men in boots in a field at dusk, holding their breath because the sky is doing a small, astonishing thing and no one else is watching.

Yaros pointed to the oaks. “Those aren’t ours,” he said. “They were old when our fathers’ fathers were boys. But we talked to them,” he said, and I thought of my mother, and I did not say, There is no magic. Instead, I thought, The air is doing math on us all, and what we call a spell is only how it feels when the sum comes out right.

When we had what the jar would give us, I closed it and bound it with string. I made duplicates with the glass vials. I made marks in my notebook that would make no sense to me a day later, not because I could not read my handwriting but because I had written down more than could be written. The air did not change but I did. I had the thing I had not wanted to have. The capture felt like a betrayal and a piece of news: the war is here, I wrote, as if we did not all know.

On the way back, the sky fanned pink over the ridge. Yaros said his mother used to call it sailor’s delight, and then we both said the rest of it, the bad part, twice in a row, for no reason except that saying things makes them real and sometimes that is what you need. At the town office, we signed a paper that said the jar and its contents belonged to the museum. Yaros put his hand on it and did not look at me. “Don’t let them sell it,” he said, which is what the letter had meant, really, all along.

In the lab, Nia, my assistant, grinned the grin of someone who has been waiting all week for you to come home. She had bound her hair with string, bright red like the one around the ceramic jar. “Did you get it?” she said, like she already knew I had.

“We got a version,” I said, because there is no The of anything here. There is only the thing that lives in this bell jar, on this day, with this human standing before it.

She set the fibers in their machine. The machine hummed and sung its neon song. Peaks rose on a screen, stalagmites of information. Nia read their labels the way a fortune-teller reads palms. I waited for science to make me less anxious. It did, in the way a doctor taking your pulse does—it’s nice to be seen, it reassures you that you’re not just air—but it did not repair what the knowing had done. I had a jar of a breath, and I did not know yet who it was for.

The Board wanted it to be for them. They had been very patient with the ethos of a small institution run by a person with principles. They had nurtured attention and not profit as long as they could. The benefactor was dead, which is when a benefactor’s money changes its body and begins to roam the room in search of other hands. There was a new company in town, with an app that could pipe a scent through the vents of their brand of smart homes and queue it for you like a playlist. Their rep came to me in a coat that cost more than my rent and said, “We think you are a national treasure.” Which is, in our city, a way of beginning to say that your vintage has reached a point where they should put you under a bell jar.

“We propose,” the rep said, “that you partner with us. Controlled releases. There is a hunger for authenticity.”

“There is hunger,” I said. “The authenticity I can’t control.”

“We’ll fund your acquisitions. We’ll modernize your stacks. We’ll make an archive in the cloud,” he said, without irony, and I thought, Fair enough; there is a cloud and it does need an archive, and also, nothing good comes in a box from a cloud when it comes to things that live in air.

“Not this one,” I said. “This one is not for sale.”

“We are only leasing the experience,” he said, offended. It is a trick of our time to call it leasing when what you’re doing is eating something that you can’t chew back again.

I brought Nia the jar. “We’re going to do a public release,” I said. She looked at me over the top of her mask as if she could see the tremor in my idea. She is twenty-seven and wise in all the ways that make the world not fall down. “It’ll be a thousand people,” I said, like this was a small number. “Maybe more.”

“People will crack open right here,” she said. “They’ll fall apart.”

“Some will,” I said. “Some won’t. Some will be disappointed.”

“We’ll lose it,” she said, looking at the jar.

“Yes,” I said. “That is what it is to give a thing to a world that can’t hold it. It is to lose it.”

We planned. We do not unleash things here carelessly. We do not throw prisms into sunlight on a day when the room is full of people with headaches. A release is like church and like a riot and like a horse race. There are public safety issues. There are quiet issues. There is Nia saying, “We need a sign at the door: If you have trauma associated with winter, think twice. Or don’t. Or do. Maybe the sign is a mirror.” There is me going out and walking the block three times to see which way the wind runs.

We called it First Breath. I did not call it 1979, the year I first noticed my mother noticing. We wrote a paragraph about the ethics, which was half apology and half invitation. The Board was unhappy. The company rep sent an email with bullet points about “pipelines of content” and “co-branding.” I did not answer his email. I put the ceramic jar in a white box. I put the box under the old oak table in the main hall. I opened the vents.

The people came. They came with hats and without, with strollers and with canes. There were children who ran and were hushed with hands that did not yet know what they were protecting. There were men who looked like they had slept in the hall last night, because they had. There were women with coats so beautiful I wanted to eat them. There were those who had known winter intimately and those who had known it as a word. There was the boy whose father smelled like night, who stood as if he had grown a foot since we last met, which he had. He stood beside me without speaking, like a friend.

I lifted the jar. It was heavy with nothing and everything. I undid the red string. It made the small rasp that string makes. I broke the wax seal. The sound was little, like a heartbeat three rooms away. I opened the lid.

It is unhelpful to say that the room breathed. Air cannot be a person. But I will say that what I had thought would be an exhale was an inhale. Something came into the room that pulled our bodies toward it, pulled our attention like a compass needle that was tired of fluttering, and then the world became very clean and very quiet.

We hear with our noses, sometimes. I have no better way to say it. We listen with parts of our brain that do not make sentences. We felt the click of a key in a lock. We stood up straighter. And then it began, the small sounds of grief and delight. A woman’s baby opened his mouth and laughed because it was cold on his gums. A man began to speak in a language no one near him seemed to know, and then his friend laughed and said, in ours, “It’s his grandmother’s tongue,” and then cried. The boy beside me did not cry. He held his hands open in front of him, palms up, as if anything could land there, a bird, a coin, the long rope of something that has been let down a well to lift us up.

Not everyone liked it. My ethics require that I tell you this. A woman near the radiator clamped her coat inside her fists and closed her eyes very tight and left quickly. A young man swallowed into himself like a tent in a storm. A teenager looked bored and then angry and then bored again. A person with a notebook stood very still and wrote a single sentence and then closed the notebook.

People spoke to me after. People always speak, because you gave them a way in. “It was almost right,” someone said, twice, and then gave me money. “It was nothing like I remember,” someone else said, and then gripped my shoulders until I had to smile to get him to stop. “It made me remember that I don’t have to die,” one man said, who was dying. “It made me remember that I will,” another said, who was not.

We lost the jar. That is to say, we used it up. Even what we had coaxed to hold still needed to be held, and then, when we opened it, and gave it to a room that had none of it, the room drank. This is why we made duplicates. This is why we take fibers and not hands and put them away. But the ceramic jar was for the story, and the story is the thing that gets you to the glass room where the science happens, and then, once they have their jar, they no longer care about the story. They leave with their own.

The company rep called the next day to tell us we were fools and geniuses. The Board called us into their conference room and said that we had overstepped the bounds of responsible stewardship. “You should have charged,” the treasurer said. “We could have made a month’s operating budget.” Nia said, from her seat beside me, “We made something else,” which is why I asked her to work here in the first place. People slipped folded paper notes under our door, the way you do at a shrine when you are not sure who you are praying to. The notes said thank you, and you broke me, and my mother loved me, and I have never been outside the city in winter, and now I want to.

I was fired. It took a month, because there were petitions, and because firings at little institutions that have become beloved take longer than they should. The Board was gentle and not kind. They did not press me to sign a non-disclosure, because what would I disclose to whom. They did not ask me to return the scarf. They gave me cake in the break room. Nia cried, and I put my hand on the top of her head like I was smoothing something and said, “Work here as long as it is yours. Then go where your nose takes you.”

I thought I would feel bereft. I felt lighter. The trouble with being allowed to keep other people’s memories is that you begin to think you are a good person because you are so careful with them. When I closed my little rented door that night, I put the ceramic jar under my bed. I did not open it. I did not think of opening it. I slept like a person in the back pew of a church.

The boy came two weeks later, because he was the kind of person who takes numbers written on the bottom of business cards and keeps them, and then waits until what he wants to say has a proper ship to sail on. We sat on the stoop and ate apples that tasted like flower water, because that was the variety I grew now in a gutter garden with my one good hand. He told me that his father had smelled like train rails in summer, and the rubber of new shoes, and paper that had been damp and then dried. He told me he was learning to tell time by noses, and that he had walked in the night and listened for when downtown’s vents changed tune, two hours after last call.

“Do you have the jar?” he asked.

“I do,” I said, because I will not lie to a boy like that.

“Will you give it to me?” he asked.

“No,” I said, because I will not lie about that either.

He nodded. Then he said, “Can we make one?” like we were making bread. And I said, “Maybe,” like we were about to invent a holiday.

We did not make a jar of his father. We worked with drawers. I took down Freight Elevator at Shift Change and Paper That Has Been Damp and Shoe Store, 2011, and something from the drawer labeled After the Beach When the Car Windows Fog Up. He breathed. He returned jars good as coins. He cried and laughed and wiped his nose on his sleeve the way boys have always done and always will until we outlaw sleeves.

“This is close,” he said, ferreting through the drawers like a raccoon. He made his hands hold air and then let it go. “Maybe it’s better,” he said, generous beyond his years. “My father never smelled like this, not all of this at once.”

“That’s one of the lies we tell,” I said. “That what we miss was more perfect than it was. It was ordinary and that is why it is perfect.”

After he left, I took the old wool scarf from my bag. It had not been mine since I was twelve. It had been everyone’s in my mother’s house: it had wiped a bicycle seat, it had wrapped a kitten on a day the oil burner failed, it had been used as a puppet to amuse, a tourniquet for a boy’s scraped knee that bled more than seemed strictly polite. I held it to my face. It smelled like cedar shavings and candle smoke, and the indescribable stratification of a drawer that has held a family’s private weather for thirty years: moth and paper and linen and the small breath of soap from a bar that had been dry so long it quit being a thing that could wet anything.

I wanted to save it. Of course I did. This is the great temptation, to put everything under glass. But not everything needs a vault. Some things need hands and then need to go to the earth.

In spring, I went to the roof. The museum’s roof had a hive on it, because bees need all the help they can get, and because the Board liked to give jars of honey with their name on it to donors. My roof had broken terracotta and compacted dirt. I took a little lichen from a fallen oak limb that Yaros had given me wrapped in brown paper. He called it reindeer moss, which it is and is not, depending on whose book you read, and named it after the animal that needs it most and will go hungry if it goes. I tucked it in between the cracks. I watered it with shot glasses because I liked how small that made the job and how large it made the result. Some days, up there, in late afternoon, the air did a little trick. It gave me a bleached-iron taste, and the hum of things turned down just one notch. It wasn’t winter. It was a reminder that the world loves itself even when we don’t know how to love it.

Nia texted me photographs of schoolchildren sitting in a room with jars on their laps. She had made them a curriculum. She was teaching them the ethics. She was better than I was at not telling people they were wrong for wanting to own what could only be shared. The company got their partnership, eventually. It was for the drawers that no one cries over—the smell of a hardware store in 1997, brand-new compression socks, printer ink the color of night. They called it base notes for your life and sold it to people who like their days to be scored like movies. That is a thing people like. People want a life with a soundtrack. People want to be told that even their garbage is meaningful. I do not despise them for this; I have a drawer of my own garbage and I open it and breathe when I am lonely and it works on me too.

The ceramic jar rested under my bed like a small sleeping animal. Sometimes at night I reached down and felt for its roundness. I did not open it. I did not need to. The people who had gathered around it on that day in the hall carried it away on their shoulders, not its contents but its form: this is what it is like to remember together, they carried. This is what it is like to be quiet so that a thing can speak.

I go back to the valley sometimes, in mud time. The development is there, of course. They named the street for the oaks they cut, as every city does: a little haunted gesture. Yaros keeps bees now, up near the cranberry bog. He kisses me on both cheeks when we meet and talks to me about weather in the way old men talk about weather when by weather they mean fate. The river is still there. It does a good job of pretending it needed no one’s help.

There is a day, every year in late January, when the city almost remembers. You can stand at the corner of my block and see your breath, and the sky has an edge you can taste, and the radiators in old buildings chatter as if they are cold and gossiping. The vents at the factory stop sighing something and begin sighing something else. The pigeons puff themselves into elegant balls. And someone, somewhere, opens a door and sticks their face out and says, It’s going to snow. People laugh. They are wrong. It will not snow. It is a trick of air and ions and the invisible mathematics of sun on brick. It is the city remembering to lift its chin and breathe.

The boy writes me letters. He is a poet now, which means that he is learning how to siphon air into lines and then give it away. He tells me that the night has more layers than his father knew, and that some of them belong to other people and you can smell them if you stand on a fire escape at three in the morning and keep still. He tells me he has learned to be quiet when other people are not and to make noise when quiet is a lie. He sends me a kerchief every winter, bundled up with grocery twine, and asks me to send it back without washing it, because it has caught a year of living and he wants to keep its breath. I do what he asks. I am a curator without a museum. That is a freer thing than I had expected.

On the first day of the first winter after I lost my job, it snowed. Real snow, from the sky, with flakes like lace. I watched it come down from the roof, and the lichen drank it in, and the city did what it always does when it snows after a long drought of it—became young, and idiotic, and brave. People threw snowballs. People skated on sidewalks. People put their fat dogs in sweaters and those dogs forgave them. A woman in a puffer coat made a perfect snow angel and then lay there too long, like a person in a painting. I walked down the stairs and opened my door and stuck my face out into the alley and said, to no one, to everyone, “It’s going to snow,” and then it did, and that is not magic but it is close, and it is enough.

content.text:
The museum does not look like a museum. If you arrive by way of the low brick warehouse between the canal and the old tram depot, you will not find a gilded entrance or a map kiosk, no gift shop with postcards or enamel pins. You will find a door with a brass plate stamped with a name that changes as often as the weather—Institution for the Preservation of Transient Phenomena, Archive of Volatiles, the Smellery, depending on the mood of the Board and which young designer had their way this season. The door is rarely locked, because locks do nothing to deter true thieves, and the things we keep here can’t be pocketed like coins. Loss here is different. It is the loss of a roomful of air, the last breath of a winter, the way a city turned its face to the sea and inhaled once, around sundown, in 1986.

I am the one who keeps it.

Or I was, for a long time. For years, my hands were the ones that lifted ampoules from their nests, that sealed them with a tiny blue flame, that etched a code in wax on glass and laid it carefully in a drawer in the cool dark. If language is the house of being, as some philosopher said, then smell is the hidden garden behind it, all the gates ajar when you stumble upon them, and feral things moving in the shadows.

People come here to cry. They come to laugh sometimes, or to stand with their hands hovering, not yet ready to break the thin meniscus of cistern air that holds a summer afternoon on a grandfather’s porch or the interior of a photo lab in a basement, with trays of stop bath and the under-scent of rubber tubing. They come to see if what they remember is anything like what the world once was. They come to learn what a thing smelled like that they only heard about in old books: whale oil lamps, coal mornings, horse and iron and snow. And they leave with their own air around them changed, utterly mundane in the ways it still is—diesel, frying onions, street gutters, bleach—but cadenced differently, as if a new instrument has tuned itself just under their hearing.

On my first day as curator, the previous keeper presented me with a towel-wrapped bundle and said, “Do not drop this.” He had never been dramatic before. Inside the towel was a little ceramic jar, white as a tooth, waxed shut, and sealed again with a tiny length of red string, the kind that used to bind pastry boxes in a bakery that existed before wheat was milled for hunger and not joy. He put the jar in my hand and looked at me until I looked back.

“What is it?” I asked.

“Late winter, under the old oaks,” he said. “After the first thaw, and then the freeze again. Lichen and wet bark and the iron in the river. Listen,” he said. “You can feel the quiet of it through the porcelain.”

I laughed. Then I stopped. Of course he was right. The thing about smell is that it never really is what it is. It is always the thing-plus. It is a chord. Your brain does the work—forging the bridge between chemical patterns and memories, laying down roads and then forgetting you laid them, so you find yourself driving at dusk and suddenly, despite every reasonable fact, a place you haven’t been in twenty years is upon you, the road rising to meet it, just from the way someone in the back seat opened a jar of peppercorns.

The museum began as a joke, as so many serious things do. There were some chemists and some poets and a benefactor who had grown rich on fragrances—that is, on made smells, fantasies of citrus blossom and clean musk—and he had the idea that there should be a counterweight to fantasy, a place that collected the actual, before the actual slipped away. This was on the cusp, when no one was paying attention to ways of life that were about to end. There was a little listicle then, that people passed around and forgot, entitled Ten Smells Our Children Will Never Know. No one thought there would be a hundred, and then a thousand. No one thought we would begin recording what streets smelled like at certain hours because those hours would be taken by other kinds of time. No one thought we would have to explain that air used to smell like nothing, or like everything, depending on the day and your mother’s hand in yours.

We learned how to take air and make it hold still. Not the air itself, which is impossible and was never the point. We learned how to coax molecules out of it, to make them nestle in other things that would not let them slip away so quickly. We used little metal needles and special fibers and chilled glass. We could give you the exhale of old books—a mix of vanillin from lignin breaking down, a hint of benzaldehyde, that dusty note I only learned to call coumarin after the head chemist brought her plant handbook to lunch and made us sniff crushed tonka beans. We could give you the upstairs hallway of a farmhouse when boots were stacked on the mat and mittens hung on the line to dry, when the dog had trailed in snow and heat came up through the vent with the faint tang of iron. We could give you a boy’s pocket in the mid-1990s, if we wanted to, insofar as a pocket had a smell: synthetic grape lip balm, pennies, the vinyl of a Pokémon card sleeve, the starch of brand-new denim.

We wrote principles. We had to. The benefactor insisted on ethics, as if this were medicine and not art. You do not capture what still breathes in the ordinary—no stealing what is abundant, for the terror of absence will corrode your hands and make a museum greedier than a dragon. And you do not counterfeit. You do not suggest that you have held, in glass, the first breath of the first lover in the first gaze. Nostalgia is a thief with bright teeth; don’t hand it a crown. You clear the names of the dead where you can, because their kitchens live on in their grandchildren’s grief, and grief belongs to people, not to institutions. You say when you have failed, and you enshrine your failures, because sometimes the most honest thing you can say about a lost thing is that you missed it by an hour and the fog burned off and your jar filled only with air.

People wrote in. They sent us handkerchiefs, scarves, clods of dirt wrapped in waxed paper. They sent us hatchlings of their childhood rooms: a broken hairbrush with their mother’s hair still braided through, a kitchen timer shaped like a tomato, letter paper with a pale cloud of perfume that hung on for decades because it had been shut away from the sun in an armoire. Sometimes we sent those things back after recording them. Sometimes we kept them under legal agreements, in the dark in the stacks, where the air hums at a temperature that is less a number than a truce. Our cataloging system would have horrified a librarian. It was intuitive the way a spice rack is intuitive. We had drawers labeled Rain on Copper, Paper in Sun, Plastic When It Was Still Called Bakelite. We had a drawer, deep and locked, labeled War.

Once, a woman in a green scarf came to the desk and said, “Do you have 2008? The smell when you walked into a store and there were flat-screen televisions everywhere all showing the same scene of a mountain?” I had to ask her three times what she meant. When I did understand, I brought her to C-14, Electronics in the Age of Awe. I gave her the box of OLED. She did not cry. She gave a dry little laugh and then she sat on the bench and breathed for a long time, shoulders dropped, like someone who had been sprinting without knowing it.

The week after, a boy came. He could not have been more than nine. He stood there with fringe in his eyes and his hands jammed into his pockets like he might keep himself from falling out of them. “My father,” he said, serious as his shoes.

“I’m sorry,” I said, before he said what. When people here say a thing without saying it, it is usually about who is gone.

“He smelled like… I can’t say,” the boy said. “He worked nights. He said the night smelled different, that it had layers like a cake, and he could tell the time by noses. He would come home and he smelled like… like handles and something like new water,” the boy said, and I nodded, because yes, yes, I knew what he meant. He meant aluminum dawn and dehumidifiers and the faint ozone that oak trees give off when there is a storm somewhere else.

“We can’t give you your father,” I said. Ethics. We can give you a hallway that people like your father walked down at three in the morning that summer, and what that hallway smelled like. We can give you the keystones.

“I know,” he said. He was braver than I was, at nine.

I gave him the jar labeled Freight Elevator at Shift Change, Summer High Humidity. The museum knows its people. The boy smiled for the first time then, a slow unwinding of something wound too tight. He breathed. He left the jar closed. He took it home in his backpack like a lantern.

The core of everything I did was attention. The method was only method. If you want method, you can read the papers. There are general principles—do not breathe on the sample, no perfume on skin, do not let a banana sit in your bag if you are going to capture the smell of old coins; be aware that clothes carry laundry detergent like a flag, that a room in summer will offer up itself differently than in winter. There are special mousetraps for stray molecules. There is glass that holds the weightless as if it weighed something. There are little boxes of silica to keep water out. There are cold places and dark places, and places where both joy and rot sleep. And there is, always, the knowledge that what you do is an act of translation. When I opened a jar for someone, I never said, “This is the thing.” I said, “This is a way in.”

The trouble was winter. Always winter. Summers morphed, but they kept the rhythm of their rot and blossom. Autumns went rusty and damp and strange, but leaves still did what leaves do, and cinnamon hung at doorways in every language. Springs packed themselves into weeks and came at you all at once like a crowd that had been waiting between velvet ropes. But winter was the one that got thin, like a bedspread stretched to cover too many feet.

When I was a child, winter had several sub-winter smells. Early winter was wool and breath and static and cedar. Deep winter smelled like metal and pine resin under its bark and the particular silence when your eyelashes freeze and crack a little and every small sound is magnified because the air says, Everything that lives is sleeping, shh. Thaw smelled like something you never did get to catch, because by the time you turned around to ask for a jar it was gone. And then there was the smell of snow itself, that everyone insists has no smell, the way people insist the number zero is nothing when it is something vast.

My mother would open the door and put her face out and say, “It’s going to snow.” And then, very soon, it would. There is science for this, which is not magic but is close; there is a mix of ions and airborne particles and an absence of the world’s usual stink. There is the smell of traffic going quiet because everyone went home. There is a faint tang of ozone, and of some things that are always present but not always available to us—wet stone, the breath of things that the cold has sealed shut.

I had a jar on my desk the whole of my tenure. It was a ceramic one, like the one my predecessor had given me, but larger, with no label. The jar was empty. I kept it as a joke and as a talisman and as a reminder that, sometimes, the closest thing to being honest is leaving the room empty, and letting people stand there with their good memories and their bad ones, and not adding one mote to impress them.

I held off on trying to capture winter for as long as I could. People brought us their own attempts, and we did what we could with them. But I knew that one day I would have to go back to the place where my mother had opened doors to sniff the sky. And then the letter came.

It was handwritten, on paper that smelled like municipal storage: a faint goatishness and something like pencil eraser. The return address was the town office of the valley where my mother kept a house after my father left, in the shadow of the old sawmill. The letter was from a man whose name I recognized only when I read it twice. He had dated the librarian’s daughter when I was twelve and then gone to fish offshore and then come back and then disappeared again into the quiet industries of men who repair what other men break.

He said: There’s a week. Maybe two. The bog is going to be taken for the development, and the oaks we called the Listening Oaks are flagged for cutting. The weather has been false all winter, but last night it froze properly, and the river speaks like it used to. If you’re going to do it, do it now.

I showed the letter to the Board. They wrung their hands and talked about liability. I put the red string around the ceramic jar and threw two sets of fibers, three vials, and the old wool scarf I had not worn in years into my bag, and I went.

The valley was thinner than I remembered. You live long enough and everything becomes thinner: the soup, your patience, the air between people and the things they mean. The river was there. It was smaller. It lay like a mirror someone had breathed on and forgot to wipe clean. The mill was a ghost. The oaks were there, their bark like plated armor, the kind that would have revived the spirits of children who liked dragons. The lichen on them was still bright, like the crusts of pastry no one makes anymore, because it takes three days and butter is reserved for candles now.

Yaros, the man with the letter, met me at the edge of the field where he’d played baseball as a boy. “You got thin,” he said, by way of greeting.

“I always was,” I said, which was true. It was the world that had thickened and thinned around me in waves, not me.

We went to the river. It was a wrinkle of iron. The air was thirty-two degrees, then twenty-nine, then something like a note struck too high. He stopped me with a hand and said, “Listen to it.”

I did. The oak boughs creaked, not like anything was breaking but like something was realigning. Somewhere far off, someone’s door banged shut. Under everything was the muffled hiss that only snow knows. And there, there, it was, the thing that my mother called winter’s breath. The air leaned in like a person at a party who wants to tell you a secret. It smelled like nickel and clean paper and the unbred parts of a forest. It smelled like the curl of a wool mitten when it’s brand new, and not the way I had worn them as a child, soaked through and dried by the stove, over and over, until they belonged to the house as much as to me.

“Now,” Yaros said.

We set the fibers in the jar. We did not talk. When people talk about reverence, they think of churches. They do not think of men in boots in a field at dusk, holding their breath because the sky is doing a small, astonishing thing and no one else is watching.

Yaros pointed to the oaks. “Those aren’t ours,” he said. “They were old when our fathers’ fathers were boys. But we talked to them,” he said, and I thought of my mother, and I did not say, There is no magic. Instead, I thought, The air is doing math on us all, and what we call a spell is only how it feels when the sum comes out right.

When we had what the jar would give us, I closed it and bound it with string. I made duplicates with the glass vials. I made marks in my notebook that would make no sense to me a day later, not because I could not read my handwriting but because I had written down more than could be written. The air did not change but I did. I had the thing I had not wanted to have. The capture felt like a betrayal and a piece of news: the war is here, I wrote, as if we did not all know.

On the way back, the sky fanned pink over the ridge. Yaros said his mother used to call it sailor’s delight, and then we both said the rest of it, the bad part, twice in a row, for no reason except that saying things makes them real and sometimes that is what you need. At the town office, we signed a paper that said the jar and its contents belonged to the museum. Yaros put his hand on it and did not look at me. “Don’t let them sell it,” he said, which is what the letter had meant, really, all along.

In the lab, Nia, my assistant, grinned the grin of someone who has been waiting all week for you to come home. She had bound her hair with string, bright red like the one around the ceramic jar. “Did you get it?” she said, like she already knew I had.

“We got a version,” I said, because there is no The of anything here. There is only the thing that lives in this bell jar, on this day, with this human standing before it.

She set the fibers in their machine. The machine hummed and sung its neon song. Peaks rose on a screen, stalagmites of information. Nia read their labels the way a fortune-teller reads palms. I waited for science to make me less anxious. It did, in the way a doctor taking your pulse does—it’s nice to be seen, it reassures you that you’re not just air—but it did not repair what the knowing had done. I had a jar of a breath, and I did not know yet who it was for.

The Board wanted it to be for them. They had been very patient with the ethos of a small institution run by a person with principles. They had nurtured attention and not profit as long as they could. The benefactor was dead, which is when a benefactor’s money changes its body and begins to roam the room in search of other hands. There was a new company in town, with an app that could pipe a scent through the vents of their brand of smart homes and queue it for you like a playlist. Their rep came to me in a coat that cost more than my rent and said, “We think you are a national treasure.” Which is, in our city, a way of beginning to say that your vintage has reached a point where they should put you under a bell jar.

“We propose,” the rep said, “that you partner with us. Controlled releases. There is a hunger for authenticity.”

“There is hunger,” I said. “The authenticity I can’t control.”

“We’ll fund your acquisitions. We’ll modernize your stacks. We’ll make an archive in the cloud,” he said, without irony, and I thought, Fair enough; there is a cloud and it does need an archive, and also, nothing good comes in a box from a cloud when it comes to things that live in air.

“Not this one,” I said. “This one is not for sale.”

“We are only leasing the experience,” he said, offended. It is a trick of our time to call it leasing when what you’re doing is eating something that you can’t chew back again.

I brought Nia the jar. “We’re going to do a public release,” I said. She looked at me over the top of her mask as if she could see the tremor in my idea. She is twenty-seven and wise in all the ways that make the world not fall down. “It’ll be a thousand people,” I said, like this was a small number. “Maybe more.”

“People will crack open right here,” she said. “They’ll fall apart.”

“Some will,” I said. “Some won’t. Some will be disappointed.”

“We’ll lose it,” she said, looking at the jar.

“Yes,” I said. “That is what it is to give a thing to a world that can’t hold it. It is to lose it.”

We planned. We do not unleash things here carelessly. We do not throw prisms into sunlight on a day when the room is full of people with headaches. A release is like church and like a riot and like a horse race. There are public safety issues. There are quiet issues. There is Nia saying, “We need a sign at the door: If you have trauma associated with winter, think twice. Or don’t. Or do. Maybe the sign is a mirror.” There is me going out and walking the block three times to see which way the wind runs.

We called it First Breath. I did not call it 1979, the year I first noticed my mother noticing. We wrote a paragraph about the ethics, which was half apology and half invitation. The Board was unhappy. The company rep sent an email with bullet points about “pipelines of content” and “co-branding.” I did not answer his email. I put the ceramic jar in a white box. I put the box under the old oak table in the main hall. I opened the vents.

The people came. They came with hats and without, with strollers and with canes. There were children who ran and were hushed with hands that did not yet know what they were protecting. There were men who looked like they had slept in the hall last night, because they had. There were women with coats so beautiful I wanted to eat them. There were those who had known winter intimately and those who had known it as a word. There was the boy whose father smelled like night, who stood as if he had grown a foot since we last met, which he had. He stood beside me without speaking, like a friend.

I lifted the jar. It was heavy with nothing and everything. I undid the red string. It made the small rasp that string makes. I broke the wax seal. The sound was little, like a heartbeat three rooms away. I opened the lid.

It is unhelpful to say that the room breathed. Air cannot be a person. But I will say that what I had thought would be an exhale was an inhale. Something came into the room that pulled our bodies toward it, pulled our attention like a compass needle that was tired of fluttering, and then the world became very clean and very quiet.

We hear with our noses, sometimes. I have no better way to say it. We listen with parts of our brain that do not make sentences. We felt the click of a key in a lock. We stood up straighter. And then it began, the small sounds of grief and delight. A woman’s baby opened his mouth and laughed because it was cold on his gums. A man began to speak in a language no one near him seemed to know, and then his friend laughed and said, in ours, “It’s his grandmother’s tongue,” and then cried. The boy beside me did not cry. He held his hands open in front of him, palms up, as if anything could land there, a bird, a coin, the long rope of something that has been let down a well to lift us up.

Not everyone liked it. My ethics require that I tell you this. A woman near the radiator clamped her coat inside her fists and closed her eyes very tight and left quickly. A young man swallowed into himself like a tent in a storm. A teenager looked bored and then angry and then bored again. A person with a notebook stood very still and wrote a single sentence and then closed the notebook.

People spoke to me after. People always speak, because you gave them a way in. “It was almost right,” someone said, twice, and then gave me money. “It was nothing like I remember,” someone else said, and then gripped my shoulders until I had to smile to get him to stop. “It made me remember that I don’t have to die,” one man said, who was dying. “It made me remember that I will,” another said, who was not.

We lost the jar. That is to say, we used it up. Even what we had coaxed to hold still needed to be held, and then, when we opened it, and gave it to a room that had none of it, the room drank. This is why we made duplicates. This is why we take fibers and not hands and put them away. But the ceramic jar was for the story, and the story is the thing that gets you to the glass room where the science happens, and then, once they have their jar, they no longer care about the story. They leave with their own.

The company rep called the next day to tell us we were fools and geniuses. The Board called us into their conference room and said that we had overstepped the bounds of responsible stewardship. “You should have charged,” the treasurer said. “We could have made a month’s operating budget.” Nia said, from her seat beside me, “We made something else,” which is why I asked her to work here in the first place. People slipped folded paper notes under our door, the way you do at a shrine when you are not sure who you are praying to. The notes said thank you, and you broke me, and my mother loved me, and I have never been outside the city in winter, and now I want to.

I was fired. It took a month, because there were petitions, and because firings at little institutions that have become beloved take longer than they should. The Board was gentle and not kind. They did not press me to sign a non-disclosure, because what would I disclose to whom. They did not ask me to return the scarf. They gave me cake in the break room. Nia cried, and I put my hand on the top of her head like I was smoothing something and said, “Work here as long as it is yours. Then go where your nose takes you.”

I thought I would feel bereft. I felt lighter. The trouble with being allowed to keep other people’s memories is that you begin to think you are a good person because you are so careful with them. When I closed my little rented door that night, I put the ceramic jar under my bed. I did not open it. I did not think of opening it. I slept like a person in the back pew of a church.

The boy came two weeks later, because he was the kind of person who takes numbers written on the bottom of business cards and keeps them, and then waits until what he wants to say has a proper ship to sail on. We sat on the stoop and ate apples that tasted like flower water, because that was the variety I grew now in a gutter garden with my one good hand. He told me that his father had smelled like train rails in summer, and the rubber of new shoes, and paper that had been damp and then dried. He told me he was learning to tell time by noses, and that he had walked in the night and listened for when downtown’s vents changed tune, two hours after last call.

“Do you have the jar?” he asked.

“I do,” I said, because I will not lie to a boy like that.

“Will you give it to me?” he asked.

“No,” I said, because I will not lie about that either.

He nodded. Then he said, “Can we make one?” like we were making bread. And I said, “Maybe,” like we were about to invent a holiday.

We did not make a jar of his father. We worked with drawers. I took down Freight Elevator at Shift Change and Paper That Has Been Damp and Shoe Store, 2011, and something from the drawer labeled After the Beach When the Car Windows Fog Up. He breathed. He returned jars good as coins. He cried and laughed and wiped his nose on his sleeve the way boys have always done and always will until we outlaw sleeves.

“This is close,” he said, ferreting through the drawers like a raccoon. He made his hands hold air and then let it go. “Maybe it’s better,” he said, generous beyond his years. “My father never smelled like this, not all of this at once.”

“That’s one of the lies we tell,” I said. “That what we miss was more perfect than it was. It was ordinary and that is why it is perfect.”

After he left, I took the old wool scarf from my bag. It had not been mine since I was twelve. It had been everyone’s in my mother’s house: it had wiped a bicycle seat, it had wrapped a kitten on a day the oil burner failed, it had been used as a puppet to amuse, a tourniquet for a boy’s scraped knee that bled more than seemed strictly polite. I held it to my face. It smelled like cedar shavings and candle smoke, and the indescribable stratification of a drawer that has held a family’s private weather for thirty years: moth and paper and linen and the small breath of soap from a bar that had been dry so long it quit being a thing that could wet anything.

I wanted to save it. Of course I did. This is the great temptation, to put everything under glass. But not everything needs a vault. Some things need hands and then need to go to the earth.

In spring, I went to the roof. The museum’s roof had a hive on it, because bees need all the help they can get, and because the Board liked to give jars of honey with their name on it to donors. My roof had broken terracotta and compacted dirt. I took a little lichen from a fallen oak limb that Yaros had given me wrapped in brown paper. He called it reindeer moss, which it is and is not, depending on whose book you read, and named it after the animal that needs it most and will go hungry if it goes. I tucked it in between the cracks. I watered it with shot glasses because I liked how small that made the job and how large it made the result. Some days, up there, in late afternoon, the air did a little trick. It gave me a bleached-iron taste, and the hum of things turned down just one notch. It wasn’t winter. It was a reminder that the world loves itself even when we don’t know how to love it.

Nia texted me photographs of schoolchildren sitting in a room with jars on their laps. She had made them a curriculum. She was teaching them the ethics. She was better than I was at not telling people they were wrong for wanting to own what could only be shared. The company got their partnership, eventually. It was for the drawers that no one cries over—the smell of a hardware store in 1997, brand-new compression socks, printer ink the color of night. They called it base notes for your life and sold it to people who like their days to be scored like movies. That is a thing people like. People want a life with a soundtrack. People want to be told that even their garbage is meaningful. I do not despise them for this; I have a drawer of my own garbage and I open it and breathe when I am lonely and it works on me too.

The ceramic jar rested under my bed like a small sleeping animal. Sometimes at night I reached down and felt for its roundness. I did not open it. I did not need to. The people who had gathered around it on that day in the hall carried it away on their shoulders, not its contents but its form: this is what it is like to remember together, they carried. This is what it is like to be quiet so that a thing can speak.

I go back to the valley sometimes, in mud time. The development is there, of course. They named the street for the oaks they cut, as every city does: a little haunted gesture. Yaros keeps bees now, up near the cranberry bog. He kisses me on both cheeks when we meet and talks to me about weather in the way old men talk about weather when by weather they mean fate. The river is still there. It does a good job of pretending it needed no one’s help.

There is a day, every year in late January, when the city almost remembers. You can stand at the corner of my block and see your breath, and the sky has an edge you can taste, and the radiators in old buildings chatter as if they are cold and gossiping. The vents at the factory stop sighing something and begin sighing something else. The pigeons puff themselves into elegant balls. And someone, somewhere, opens a door and sticks their face out and says, It’s going to snow. People laugh. They are wrong. It will not snow. It is a trick of air and ions and the invisible mathematics of sun on brick. It is the city remembering to lift its chin and breathe.

The boy writes me letters. He is a poet now, which means that he is learning how to siphon air into lines and then give it away. He tells me that the night has more layers than his father knew, and that some of them belong to other people and you can smell them if you stand on a fire escape at three in the morning and keep still. He tells me he has learned to be quiet when other people are not and to make noise when quiet is a lie. He sends me a kerchief every winter, bundled up with grocery twine, and asks me to send it back without washing it, because it has caught a year of living and he wants to keep its breath. I do what he asks. I am a curator without a museum. That is a freer thing than I had expected.

On the first day of the first winter after I lost my job, it snowed. Real snow, from the sky, with flakes like lace. I watched it come down from the roof, and the lichen drank it in, and the city did what it always does when it snows after a long drought of it—became young, and idiotic, and brave. People threw snowballs. People skated on sidewalks. People put their fat dogs in sweaters and those dogs forgave them. A woman in a puffer coat made a perfect snow angel and then lay there too long, like a person in a painting. I walked down the stairs and opened my door and stuck my face out into the alley and said, to no one, to everyone, “It’s going to snow,” and then it did, and that is not magic but it is close, and it is enough.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
